1.1: I, Paul, am an apostle of Christ Jesus—appointed by God's own will—sent to proclaim the life that is found in Christ Jesus.
1.2: To Timothy, my dear son in the faith: May God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord give you grace, mercy, and peace.
1.3: I thank God—the same God my ancestors served, whom I serve with a clean conscience—every time I remember you in my prayers, which is constantly, night and day.
1.4: I remember your tears, and I long to see you so I can be filled with joy.
1.5: I keep thinking about your genuine, unfeigned faith—faith that first took root in your grandmother Lois and your mother Eunice, and I am fully confident it now lives in you as well.
1.6: This is why I urge you: stir up into full flame the gift that God placed in you when I laid my hands on you.
1.7: God did not give us a Spirit that makes us shrink back in fear. He gave us a Spirit of power, love, and self-control.
1.8: So do not be ashamed to bear witness to our Lord, or to stand beside me his prisoner. Join me instead in suffering for the gospel—and do it in the power God provides.
1.9: This is the God who saved us and called us to a holy life—not because of our deeds, but because of his own purpose and grace. This grace was given to us in Christ Jesus before the world began,
1.10: and it has now burst into the open through the appearing of our Savior Christ Jesus. He has broken the power of death and flooded everything with the light of life and immortality through the gospel.
1.11: I was appointed to this gospel as its herald, apostle, and teacher.
1.12: This is why I am suffering as I do—yet I am not ashamed. I know exactly whom I have trusted, and I am absolutely convinced that he is able to guard everything I have placed in his care until the last day.
1.13: Hold firmly to the pattern of sound teaching you received from me—hold it in the faith and love that are yours in Christ Jesus.
1.14: Guard the precious deposit entrusted to you. You can do it—the Holy Spirit who dwells in us will help you.
1.15: You know that everyone in the province of Asia has turned their back on me—Phygelus and Hermogenes among them.
1.16: May the Lord show mercy to the family of Onesiphorus. He repeatedly lifted my spirits and was never embarrassed by my chains.
1.17: On the contrary, when he came to Rome, he searched for me persistently until he tracked me down.
1.18: May the Lord grant that he will find mercy from the Lord on that final day. And you know better than anyone how much he served me in Ephesus.

2.1: So then, my son, draw your strength from the grace that is yours in Christ Jesus.
2.2: What you heard from me in front of many witnesses—pass that on to trustworthy people who will, in turn, be able to teach others.
2.3: Take your share of suffering alongside me, as a good soldier of Christ Jesus.
2.4: A soldier on active duty doesn't get tangled up in the business of ordinary life—his whole aim is to please the officer who enlisted him.
2.5: And an athlete doesn't receive the winner's wreath unless he competes by the rules.
2.6: The farmer who does the hard work deserves to be the first to eat from his harvest.
2.7: Think carefully about what I'm telling you. The Lord will help you see what it means.
2.8: Keep this at the center of everything: Jesus Christ—raised from the dead, a descendant of David. This is the gospel I proclaim,
2.9: for which I suffer like a criminal—yes, even in chains. But God's word cannot be chained.
2.10: So I endure everything for the sake of those God has chosen—so they too can obtain the salvation that is in Christ Jesus, with all its eternal glory.
2.11: This is a trustworthy saying: If we have died with him, we will also live with him.
2.12: If we endure, we will reign with him. If we disown him, he will disown us.
2.13: Even when we fail him, he remains faithful—he cannot contradict his own nature.
2.14: Keep putting these truths before people. Charge them solemnly before God to stop quarreling over words—it serves no purpose and only tears apart those who listen.
2.15: Work hard so you can stand before God without shame—a workman who correctly cuts the word of truth straight.
2.16: Steer clear of godless, empty talk. Those who indulge in it only sink deeper and deeper into ungodliness.
2.17: Their words spread like gangrene. Hymenaeus and Philetus are examples of this—
2.18: they have swerved away from the truth by claiming the resurrection has already happened, and they are capsizing the faith of some people.
2.19: Yet God's solid foundation cannot be shaken. It bears this double seal: 'The Lord knows those who belong to him'—an echo of Korah's rebellion, when God vindicated his own—and: 'Everyone who claims the Lord's name must turn away from evil.'
2.20: In a large household you don't find only gold and silver bowls—there are also wooden and clay ones. Some are set apart for honorable use; others are used for everyday, disposable tasks.
2.21: If you purge yourself of the dishonorable things, you become a vessel set apart for honorable use—made holy, useful to the Master, ready for any good work.
2.22: Run from youthful passions. Instead, pursue righteousness, faith, love, and peace—alongside everyone who calls on the Lord with a sincere heart.
2.23: Have nothing to do with foolish, uninformed arguments—you already know they only breed fights.
2.24: The Lord's servant must not be a fighter. He must be gentle with everyone, skilled at teaching, able to bear wrongs without bitterness.
2.25: He must gently instruct those who push back—because God may grant them the repentance they need to come to the knowledge of the truth.
2.26: Then they can sober up and escape the devil's trap—they have been captured by him to serve his purposes, but God can free them.

3.1: Understand this clearly: in the last days perilous times will arrive.
3.2: People will be utterly self-obsessed and money-hungry—boastful, arrogant, abusive, disobedient to parents, ungrateful, and godless.
3.3: They will be loveless, unforgiving, slanderous, without self-control, savage, and hostile to everything good.
3.4: They will be treacherous, reckless, puffed up—devoted to pleasure rather than to God.
3.5: They will keep up the outward appearance of religion while emptying it of all its power. Stay away from people like this.
3.6: These are the ones who slither into households and gain power over vulnerable women—women weighed down by guilt and tossed about by one craving after another.
3.7: They are perpetually taking in information yet never arriving at the truth.
3.8: Just as Jannes and Jambres stood against Moses, these men stand against the truth. Their minds are rotten and when it comes to the faith, they have failed the test.
3.9: But they won't get far. Their stupidity will become obvious to everyone—just as it was with Jannes and Jambres.
3.10: But you have followed closely my teaching, my way of life, my purpose, my faith, my patience, my love, my endurance—
3.11: the persecutions and sufferings that came on me in Antioch, in Iconium, in Lystra. You know the kinds of things I endured—and out of all of them the Lord rescued me.
3.12: And make no mistake: everyone who intends to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will face persecution.
3.13: Meanwhile evil people and frauds will go from bad to worse—they deceive others and are themselves deceived.
3.14: But you—stand firm in what you have learned and become fully convinced of, knowing the people you learned it from.
3.15: From childhood you have known the holy Scriptures, and they have the power to make you wise for salvation—through faith in Christ Jesus.
3.16: Every Scripture text carries the very breath of God—which makes it useful for teaching the truth, exposing error, correcting faults, and training people in the way of righteousness.
3.17: This is how the person who belongs to God becomes complete—fully outfitted for every good work there is.

4.1: I charge you solemnly—before God and before Christ Jesus, who will one day judge the living and the dead, and in light of his appearing and his kingdom:
4.2: Preach the word. Be ready to do it whether the moment seems right or not. Correct, rebuke, and encourage—always with deep patience and careful teaching.
4.3: The day is coming when people will refuse to put up with sound teaching. Instead, driven by their own cravings, they will surround themselves with teachers who scratch the itch they want scratched.
4.4: They will stop listening to the truth and wander off after myths.
4.5: But you—stay clear-headed in everything. Bear hardship. Do the work of an evangelist. Carry out your ministry to the full.
4.6: For I am already being poured out like a sacrificial drink offering, and the hour of my departure has arrived.
4.7: I have fought the good fight to its end. I have run the full course. I have kept the faith.
4.8: From now on the crown of righteousness waits for me. The Lord, who judges rightly, will place it on my head on that final day—and not only on mine, but on the head of everyone who has been longing for his return.
4.9: Make every effort to come to me soon.
4.10: Demas has abandoned me—he fell in love with this present age—and gone to Thessalonica. Crescens has gone to Galatia, Titus to Dalmatia.
4.11: Only Luke is still with me. Pick up Mark on your way—he is genuinely useful to me in the work.
4.12: I have sent Tychicus to Ephesus.
4.13: When you come, bring the cloak I left at Troas with Carpus, and bring my scrolls—especially the parchments.
4.14: Alexander the metalworker caused me enormous trouble. The Lord will pay him back for what he did.
4.15: You should watch out for him too—he put up fierce resistance to our message.
4.16: When I appeared for my first hearing, no one stood with me—everyone deserted me. May it not be counted against them.
4.17: But the Lord himself stood by my side and poured strength into me—so that through me the proclamation would be completed and every nation would hear it. And I was rescued from the lion's jaws.
4.18: The Lord will rescue me from every evil assault and bring me safely home to his heavenly kingdom. To him be glory for ever and ever. Amen.
4.19: Give my greetings to Priscilla and Aquila, and to the family of Onesiphorus.
4.20: Erastus stayed behind in Corinth. I had to leave Trophimus sick in Miletus.
4.21: Try hard to get here before winter. Eubulus sends greetings, as do Pudens, Linus, Claudia, and all the brothers and sisters here.
4.22: May the Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you all.
